schools.summary: Ellicott defends the 480 as a formal epoch answering Exodus 12:40-41; Clarke lists a dozen rival reckonings but trusts the Hebrew text.
Gill accepts the 480 and itemizes the intervening periods to the exact sum.
Henry reads the long delay as deliberate: Israel had made itself unworthy, and God values no external pomp.
Clarke fixes Zif as April-May and takes the borrowed month-names as marks of a post-exilic final form.

debates.question: How long after the Exodus was the temple founded?

sides.position: the 480 years of the Hebrew text, reckoned through the wilderness, Joshua, judges, and kings
rival totals — the Septuagint's 440, Josephus's 592, and others

schools.summary: The temple exactly doubles the tabernacle (eight times its bulk) yet remains a small shrine relying on costliness, not size; measured by the sacred cubit.
Gill notes only that the house was built for the Lord's worship, honour, and glory.
Clarke reckons the cubits at nearly 22 inches but declines to reconstruct the building's disputed appearance.
The porch was as wide as the house and ten cubits deep; the 120-cubit height of 2 Chronicles 3:4 is judged a copyist's error, since such a height would be a tower, not a porch.
Gill fixes the porch at the east end, before the temple.
Keil and Ellicott read 'windows of closed beams' — fixed, immovable lattices for light and air through thick walls.
Clarke defends the margin's 'broad within, narrow without,' splayed like castle windows, and derives 'window' from 'wind-door.'
Gill takes them as windows that could be both, having shutters to open or close.
Henry moralizes the splayed lights — look much within to judge ourselves, little without to censure others — and reads their narrowness as the dimness of that dispensation.

sides.position: windows splayed broad within and narrow without
windows with fixed, immovable lattices of closed beams

themes: light and air through thick walls
the dimness of the old dispensation

schools.summary: Keil describes a three-storeyed outwork built against the two sides and rear, distinguished grammatically as whole and parts.
Gill corrects the preposition — chambers built upon, not into, the wall — and limits them to the north, south, and west, not the porch-end.

themes: beams kept from the holy wall

schools.summary: Keil explains the graded breadths by rebated ledges cut into the temple wall, so the beams rested on steppings and were not driven into the holy masonry.
Gill fixes 'the nethermost chamber' as the lowest of the three tiers.
Henry draws the church-lesson: do not impair the church's strength under pretence of adding to its beauty.

themes: care not to weaken the church

schools.summary: Keil insists the stones were fully tooled at the quarry, not miraculously unhewn; Ellicott grounds the silent reverence in the altar-law of Exodus 20:25 and Deuteronomy 27:5.
Clarke makes the noiseless building a type: souls, as living stones, must be squared and fitted here for their place in the New Jerusalem.
Henry: this was the temple of the God of peace; quietness befits religious exercises, and clamour never furthers God's work.

themes: living stones for the New Jerusalem

schools.summary: Gill places the door of the side-chambers on the south side of the house.
Keil reads the winding stair and, on the roofing, renders 'beams' not 'boards,' the covering being the roof, not the inner panelling.
Ellicott confines the cedar to the roof and denies the outer walls were wood-clad; Gill marks the fabric now finished.
Clarke explains the Eastern custom of wooden ceilings over tiled floors, citing Westminster Hall.
Keil gives each storey five cubits, so the side-building rose two-thirds up the wall, leaving room for the clerestory windows above.
Gill takes these as the upper chambers, raising the house level with the porch, and glances at the disciples after the ascension.

themes: bound but not bored into the wall

schools.summary: Keil takes 'the word of the Lord came' as a prophetic medium, God appearing to Solomon in person only twice (1 Kings 9:2).
Gill reports the Jewish tradition naming Ahijah the Shilonite as the messenger.
Clarke distinguishes this word, given during building, from the later revelation after the dedication.
Henry: none serve God without his eye on them; the promise encourages, the condition sobers — keeping commandments is better than building churches.
Keil reads the absolute construction and ties the promise to the Davidic pledge of 2 Samuel 7:12 and Solomon's charge in 1 Kings 2:3.
Gill takes the whole message as bearing directly on the fate of this very house.
Gill anchors the indwelling in the house in the midst of the land, the place of sacrifice and of prayer.
Keil denies a second covenant: the promise to dwell is a special application of the Davidic pledge, its negative side being 'I will not forsake.'
Gill reads 'finished' as deliberate completion, and cites Abarbanel that the word came mid-work lest Solomon trust the fabric's strength.
Henry: the strictness of God's government does not drive a good man off but quickens him; Solomon built and finished, and God went with him.
Henry unfolds the fourfold type: Christ the true temple, the believer a living temple, the church the mystical temple, heaven the everlasting temple.
Keil suspects 'walls of the ceiling' a copyist's slip for 'beams'; Ellicott follows the margin, LXX, and Vulgate on the cedar and cypress covering.
Gill marks the stage: the stone walls now wainscotted with cedar.
Keil canvasses the derivation of 'oracle' from the root 'to speak' — the place of the divine answer — favoring the traditional etymology.
Gill fixes the most holy place as a twenty-cubit room partitioned at the hinder end and wainscotted like the rest.

themes: the place of the divine voice

schools.summary: Keil reads the forty cubits forward into the carved cedar and the waiting ark, dividing the house into a forty-cubit Holy Place and twenty-cubit oracle.
Gill glosses 'the temple before it' as the holy place, before the most holy.
Gill notes only the cedar lining of the interior; the verse's own words carry the sense — knops and open flowers, all cedar, no stone seen.
Gill defines the oracle as the speaking-place prepared to receive the ark, the place whence the Lord gave answer.
Clarke declines to expand the picture and refers the reader to the full description appended at the chapter's end.

themes: the place of the divine answer

schools.summary: Keil renders the perfect cube and weighs the Hebrew for 'fine gold'; Ellicott corrects 'in the forepart' to 'the inner part' and reads the cedar-covered altar.
Gill takes 'the forepart' as a lower front section of the oracle, open to admit light and incense-smoke from the Holy Place.

debates.question: What does 'in the forepart' of the oracle mean?

sides.position: the inner part, the wall facing the entrance and the region behind it
a lower front section of the shrine, left open for light and smoke

schools.summary: Keil reverses an earlier view, reading the verb as bolting or fastening with golden chains; Ellicott takes a golden chain across the entrance as a further guard.
Gill reckons the gold at six hundred talents, over three million pounds sterling.
Ellicott shows the incense-altar stood in the Holy Place yet 'belonged to the oracle,' explaining the disputed word in Hebrews 9:4; Keil and Gill confirm both rooms gilded.
Clarke owns the quantity of gold impossible to calculate.

themes: the altar belonging to the oracle

schools.summary: Ellicott traces the cherubim from Eden to the Apocalypse, denies they are emblems of Deity, and suspects the temple pair were turned to face the entrance.
Gill reads the two figures as emblems of the attending angels or of the two witnesses, the olive trees of Revelation 11:4.
Keil gathers the wing-measures: each wing five cubits, the pair spanning the twenty-cubit breadth wall to wall and meeting at the centre.
Gill glosses the one wing as its span stretched out on one side.
Gill reads the ten cubits of the second cherub as its wingspan, matching the first; the verse's repetition insists the two were of one measure and size.
Gill: the height is repeated to mark an extraordinary size.
Clarke converts the ten cubits to about eighteen feet three inches, referring to his note on Exodus 25:18.
Gill sets the cherubim within the holy of holies, the room for which they were made.
Keil's reading of these verses shows the wings reaching wall to wall and meeting at the centre, the ark to rest beneath.
Gill reads the gilding of the cherubim as the purity of angels or the graces of the Spirit adorning the witnesses of Christ.
Gill reads the palm trees as upright men and the open flowers as grace in full bloom, the walls an emblem of the redeemed.
Ellicott weighs whether the carving covered all the walls or, more fitly, only the oracle and partition.
Keil fixes 'within and without' as the Most Holy and the Holy Place, both floors gilded.
Gill hears in the golden floor the street of the new Jerusalem and the holy walk of God's people.
Henry: gold was under foot, as it should be in the living temples, its abundance lessening its worth.

themes: the street of the new Jerusalem

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott read the projecting framework as a fifth part of the wall, the modest doors of the oracle usually standing open before the veil.
Gill reads the olive-wood door as typical of Christ, the true olive and the door into the church above and below.
Keil explains the gold-leaf spread to take the shape of the carving; Ellicott notes each heavy leaf folded upon itself.
Gill observes the leaves named again for the sake of their ornament.
Keil reads the framework as a fourth of the wall, the doors of cypress rather than olive — lighter and less likely to sink.
Gill identifies 'the door of the temple' as the entrance of the holy place.
Keil reads the folding leaves forward into the carving and the gold plate fastened with tacks (2 Chronicles 3:9).
Gill notes the posts of olive but the doors of fir, both folding.
Gill notes the carving was as upon the doors of the most holy place, and the gold fitted upon the carved work.

themes: the house all of a piece

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil prefer a low enclosing wall of three stone courses with cedar coping, set higher than the outer court so the people could see through; Ellicott maps the whole system of courts to 'Solomon's Porch.'
Clarke reads alternating strata of stone and timber and links it to the stone crying from the wall in Habakkuk 2:11.
Henry: even under that dispensation the people were not kept wholly in the dark or at a distance.

debates.question: Is the 'three rows of stone and a row of cedar' a raised floor or an enclosing wall?

sides.position: a low wall of three stone courses with a coping of cedar
alternating strata of stone and timber (perhaps a raised floor)

schools.summary: Keil reads the two dates as one span of seven and a half years and explains Bul as the Phoenician 'fruit month'; Ellicott glosses Zif as 'brightness of flowers,' about May.
Gill fixes the founding in the fourth year of Solomon's reign.
Henry: the first three years were spent settling the kingdom, that nothing might embarrass the work.

themes: beginning in the season of flowers

schools.summary: Keil marks how swiftly the work was done against Diana's temple at Ephesus (220 years, per Pliny); Ellicott and Gill fix the eleventh year and the month Bul.
Clarke: the ornamenting consumed the time; the temple was a place to worship at, not in, unlike the wonders of the heathen world.
Henry: Solomon was in earnest, had means and no distraction, and many hands made quick work.
